description: Still unmissable, but the cracks begin. Homer's Enemy. Trash of the Titans. The Principal and the Pauper.

episodes.title: The City of New York vs. Homer Simpson
episodes.description: Designated driver Barney absconds to Manhattan in Homer's car and leaves it there. But when the family journeys to the Big Apple to retrieve their property, Homer discovers his car has been booted.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch which is actually a street bench, and they get mugged by a man who steals the bench.
episodes.notes: This episode was pulled from syndication and streaming for years after 9/11 due to its prominent depiction of the World Trade Center towers, making it one of the most censored episodes in TV history.

episodes.title: The Principal and the Pauper
episodes.description: Unmasked as an impostor, Principal Skinner flees to Capital City to reclaim his former life as a shiftless good-for-nothing. The people of Springfield gain a newfound appreciation for him and form a plan to bring him back.
episodes.notes: Often cited as the most controversial episode in the series' history, this installment permanently altered a beloved character's backstory by revealing Principal Skinner was actually an impostor named Armin Tamzarian. The episode sparked fierce debate among fans and writers about whether long-running shows should drastically revise established character histories.

episodes.title: Lisa's Sax
episodes.description: After Lisa’s saxophone is destroyed, the Simpsons reminisce about how she originally got the instrument. On the hottest day of the year, Homer must decide whether to buy an air conditioner or replace Lisa’s sax.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets bounced high into the air, then crashes back down.
episodes.notes: This flashback episode revealed that Lisa first got her saxophone in 1990 when Marge was pregnant with Maggie, establishing crucial Simpson family timeline and explaining Lisa's musical passion through her connection with bleeding gums Murphy.

episodes.title: Treehouse of Horror VIII
episodes.description: In “The Homega Man,” Homer faces a post-apocalyptic Springfield. Next, Bart attempts to become Superfly in "Fly vs. Fly." In “Easy-Bake Coven,” amidst a witch hunt in 1649, Marge and her sisters invent trick-or-treating.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets crushed by a giant foot in a Monty Python reference.
episodes.parody_of: The Omega Man, The Fly, Salem witch trials
episodes.notes: This episode marks the first time a Treehouse of Horror segment is set in historical times rather than contemporary Springfield, with "Easy-Bake Coven" taking place in 1649 colonial America.

episodes.title: The Cartridge Family
episodes.description: Alarmed by a soccer riot, Homer buys a handgun. His reckless behavior drives Marge and the kids out of the house, and even his new gun club buddies desert him.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one.
episodes.notes: This episode introduced the NRA-parodying gun club and Homer's memorable line "Guns are for family protection, hunting dangerous or delicious animals, and keeping the King of England out of your face," which became one of the series' most quoted pro-gun satire moments.

episodes.title: Bart Star
episodes.description: When Homer becomes the coach of a peewee football team, he replaces star quarterback Nelson with the less talented Bart. Facing his teammates’ scorn, Bart looks for a way out but finds a better way to show his team spirit.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which is actually a monster's mouth that swallows them whole.
episodes.parody_of: Any Given Sunday and general sports movie tropes

episodes.title: The Two Mrs. Nahasapeemapetilons
episodes.description: Trying to escape his arranged marriage, Apu asks Marge to pose as his wife during his mother’s visit. When the ruse fails, Homer makes a last-ditch attempt to intervene, but Apu is smitten with his new bride Manjula.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets crushed by a foot, revealing they are tiny figures in a snow globe held by a giant Maggie.
episodes.notes: This episode introduces Manjula Nahasapeemapetilon, Apu's wife, who would become a recurring character and the mother of octuplets in later episodes.

episodes.title: Lisa the Skeptic
episodes.description: When a skeleton is unearthed at the site of a future shopping mall, all Springfield is convinced it is the skeleton of an angel – except Lisa.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which is actually a wooden bench in a jail cell.
episodes.parody_of: Religious hysteria and archaeological discovery stories
episodes.notes: This episode marked one of the earliest mainstream TV treatments of intelligent design debates, airing months before the concept gained widespread political attention in the late 1990s.

episodes.title: Realty Bites
episodes.description: When Marge gets her real estate license, she learns to put a positive spin on the flaws of the houses she’s selling. But when the Flanders buy the Murder House, she realizes she has to tell the truth.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions, then shot out through the back.
episodes.notes: This episode features one of the series' most memorable throwaway gags when Marge describes a house as having a "possum problem" while a family of possums casually walks by in the background. The Murder House subplot became a recurring location reference in later episodes, though it rarely appeared on screen again.

episodes.title: Miracle on Evergreen Terrace
episodes.description: After accidentally ruining the family’s Christmas tree and presents, Bart buries the evidence and claims a burglar stole everything. When Kent Brockman does a human interest story on the family’s tragic loss, the townspeople shower the family with generosity… until the truth comes out.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch as normal, but then the couch and family are revealed to be a snow globe being shaken.
episodes.parody_of: It's a Wonderful Life
episodes.notes: This episode marked one of the first times The Simpsons directly addressed the show's growing reputation for cynicism, as the townspeople's initial generosity turns to mob-like vengeance when Bart's deception is revealed.

episodes.title: All Singing, All Dancing
episodes.description: Homer rents a Clint Eastwood movie and is dismayed to find it's a musical. Using clips from past episodes, the rest of the family reminds him about the many times he and other members of the family and town have broken into song and dance.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and it transforms into a player piano with the family bouncing up and down to the music.
episodes.parody_of: Musical films and clip shows
episodes.notes: The first full musical episode of The Simpsons, featuring 13 songs compiled from previous episodes to create a cohesive clip show format. This experimental structure would influence later musical episodes like "Simpsoncalifragilisticexpiala(Annoyed Grunt)cious" and the Emmy-winning "We Put the Spring in Springfield

episodes.title: Bart Carny
episodes.description: When the carnival comes to town, Homer and Bart wind up working the ring toss. A father-son team of grifters shows them the downside of the “carny code,” but the Simpsons beat them at their own game.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions like quicksand.
episodes.notes: This episode introduced the carny code philosophy "You gotta play to win, but you also gotta win to play," which became a memorable Simpsons catchphrase among fans. The carnival setting allowed the show to explore the seedier side of traveling entertainment while showcasing Homer and Bart working together as unlikely con artists.

episodes.title: The Joy of Sect
episodes.description: A cult lures in most of Springfield to toil for “The Leader.” After Marge escapes and has the family deprogrammed, Homer exposes the man behind the spaceship.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch which turns into a monster and swallows them, then burps.
episodes.parody_of: Cults and cult deprogramming, particularly referencing Heaven's Gate and Scientology
episodes.notes: This episode aired just one year after the Heaven's Gate cult's mass suicide, making it one of the most topically immediate parodies in Simpsons history.

episodes.title: Das Bus
episodes.description: When Otto drives the school bus off a bridge, the kids wind up on a deserted island, fending for themselves in a “Lord of the Flies” parody. Back in Springfield, Homer takes on Bill Gates.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into a whirlpool that appears in the living room floor.
episodes.parody_of: Lord of the Flies
episodes.notes: The Bill Gates subplot features one of the show's most prescient tech industry parodies, with Gates portrayed as a ruthless monopolist years before Microsoft's major antitrust battles

episodes.title: The Last Temptation of Krust
episodes.description: Krusty bombs at a charity comedy event and feels like a tired old hack. With the Simpsons’ support, Krusty takes his act in a fresh new direction… but in the end, his true nature asserts itself.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one until only their eyes are visible.
episodes.parody_of: Stand-up comedy culture and alternative comedy movement of the 1990s
episodes.notes: This episode marked one of the earliest mainstream TV parodies of the alternative comedy movement, predicting the real-world backlash against "anti-comedy" performers who would later face similar sellout accusations.

episodes.title: Dumbbell Indemnity
episodes.description: Moe goes broke trying to wine and dine a new girlfriend. Desperate for money, he cooks up an insurance scam with Homer.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one.
episodes.parody_of: Double Indemnity
episodes.notes: This episode marks one of the few times The Simpsons directly parodied a classic film noir, with the title and plot structure closely following Billy Wilder's 1944 masterpiece "Double Indemnity.

episodes.title: Lisa the Simpson
episodes.description: Lisa struggles with solving a simple puzzle and fears that the "Simpson gene” is suddenly wiping out her intelligence. Homer stages a family reunion in hopes of introducing Lisa to some Simpsons she can respect.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one.
episodes.notes: This episode established the canonical explanation that the "Simpson gene" only affects male descendants, revealing that Simpson women like Lisa retain their intelligence while the men become progressively dumber with age.

episodes.title: This Little Wiggy
episodes.description: At Marge’s insistence, Bart has a “play date” with Ralph. Bart suddenly sees the upside of this friendship when he discovers Chief Wiggum’s “master key” and goes to explore Springfield’s abandoned prison.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch which turns into a monster and swallows them.
episodes.notes: Features one of Ralph Wiggum's most quoted lines when he declares "I bent my Wookiee" while playing with action figures, which became a popular internet meme years later.

episodes.title: Simpson Tide
episodes.description: When Homer joins the Naval Reserve, a captain takes a liking to him and leaves him in charge of a nuclear submarine.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch which turns into a wooden boat that sails across a stormy sea.
episodes.parody_of: The Hunt for Red October
episodes.notes: This episode features one of the series' most elaborate parodies of *The Hunt for Red October*, complete with detailed submarine sequences that required extensive research into naval procedures and terminology.

episodes.title: The Trouble with Trillions
episodes.description: Facing an IRS audit, Homer agrees to help the feds bust Mr. Burns for a missing trillion-dollar bill. When Burns makes an anti-government stand, Homer flees with him to Cuba.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the TV, appearing in various shows including a nature documentary where they're eaten by cheetahs.
episodes.parody_of: Government conspiracy thrillers and Cold War spy films
episodes.notes: This episode features one of the series' most absurd premises, with Homer literally helping Mr. Burns steal a trillion-dollar bill and flee to Cuba to meet Fidel Castro. The Castro scenes were among the most politically charged moments in the show's history, airing during the height of U.S.-Cuba tensions.

episodes.title: Girly Edition
episodes.description: Lisa becomes the anchor of a kids’ news program but is upstaged by Bart’s treacly human interest stories. At home, Homer corrupts a helper monkey.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and sinks into it like quicksand, with only their eyes visible above the cushions.
episodes.parody_of: children's news programs and human interest journalism
episodes.notes: This episode introduced Mojo, the helper monkey who becomes corrupted by Homer's lazy lifestyle, in what became one of the series' most memorable animal character storylines.

episodes.title: Trash of the Titans
episodes.description: Homer feuds with the Springfield garbage men and decides to run for Sanitation Commissioner. After winning the election with a sleazy campaign, Homer can’t keep the extravagant promises he made to the town.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the TV, where they appear in various shows including a nature documentary.
episodes.notes: The episode's campaign song "Can't Someone Else Do It?" became one of the show's most memorable political satire moments, perfectly encapsulating Homer's lazy populism. This marked one of the rare times Homer actually wins an election and faces the consequences of his unrealistic campaign promises.

episodes.title: King of the Hill
episodes.description: After Rainier Wolfcastle helps Homer get in shape, Homer accepts a challenge to climb the Murderhorn, the tallest mountain in Springfield.
episodes.parody_of: Mountain climbing films and Cliffhanger
episodes.notes: Rainier Wolfcastle's first major role outside of McBain action movie parodies, establishing him as a fully-realized character rather than just a film-within-show joke.

episodes.title: Lost Our Lisa
episodes.description: Bart’s latest stunt thwarts Lisa’s plan to attend a museum exhibit. When she tries to go alone and takes the wrong bus, Homer sets out to find her.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions, then shot out through the back.
episodes.notes: This episode features one of the series' most touching father-daughter moments when Homer tells Lisa "You make me proud," marking a rare instance of pure emotional sincerity from Homer without comedic undercut.

episodes.title: Natural Born Kissers
episodes.description: Homer and Marge try to spice up their love life and are caught baring it all in public. Bart and Lisa discover an alternate ending to Casablanca.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch which turns into a monster and swallows them.
episodes.parody_of: Casablanca
episodes.notes: This episode features one of the most extensive public nudity sequences in Simpsons history, with Homer and Marge's naked adventure spanning multiple locations including a hot air balloon ride over Springfield Stadium during a football game.

episodes.title: Lard of the Dance
episodes.description: Homer and Bart try to make money by selling used grease. Meanwhile, Lisa organizes a school dance but feels alienated when a new classmate and her friends try to act like adults.

guests.character: Milhouse Van Houten / Janey Powell / Wendell (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which is actually a toaster that launches them into the air as burnt toast.
episodes.notes: This episode marked the first time The Simpsons directly addressed the trend of children trying to act older than their age, with Lisa's storyline reflecting growing concerns about kids growing up too fast in the late 1990s.

episodes.title: The Wizard of Evergreen Terrace
episodes.description: A midlife crisis prompts Homer to quit his job and become an inventor.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one.
episodes.parody_of: Thomas Edison and famous inventors
episodes.notes: Homer's makeshift electric hammer accidentally anticipates the design of a modern cordless drill, making this one of the few times his incompetence accidentally predicted real technology.

episodes.title: Bart the Mother
episodes.description: Under Nelson's bad influence, Bart shoots a bird with a BB gun. Stricken with remorse, he tries to nurture the eggs from the bird's nest, which hatch into a species of lizard outlawed in Springfield.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one.

episodes.title: Treehouse of Horror IX
episodes.description: In "Hell Toupée," Snake is sent to the electric chair, and his hair is transplanted onto Homer. In "The Terror of Tiny Toon," Bart and Lisa become cartoon characters in an episode of Itchy & Scratchy. In "Starship Poopers," a growth spurt casts doubt on Maggie's paternity, and the family goes on the Jerry Springer show.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch as normal, but then the camera pulls back to reveal they're inside a snow globe being shaken by an alien.
episodes.parody_of: Multiple parodies: horror films, Itchy & Scratchy cartoons, Jerry Springer Show, and alien abduction stories
episodes.notes: The Jerry Springer parody segment marked one of the first times The Simpsons directly lampooned a contemporary daytime talk show, complete with Springer himself providing his own voice for the alien tribunal finale.

episodes.title: When You Dish Upon a Star
episodes.description: After a chance encounter with Hollywood celebrities secretly living in Springfield, Homer becomes their friend and gofer... until he spills the beans to the gang at Moe's.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the TV, appearing as guests on various TV shows.
episodes.parody_of: Hollywood celebrity culture and tabloid journalism
episodes.notes: Features real Hollywood celebrities Alec Baldwin, Kim Basinger, and Ron Howard playing themselves, marking one of the few times A-list stars appeared as themselves rather than fictional characters on The Simpsons.

episodes.title: D'oh-in' in the Wind
episodes.description: In a quest to learn Homer's middle name, Homer and Grandpa travel to his mother's old commune, where Homer embraces the hippie life.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which is actually a spider that wraps them up in a web.
episodes.parody_of: 1960s hippie counterculture and commune lifestyle
episodes.notes: This episode reveals Homer's middle name as Jay, finally answering a question that had lingered since the show's early seasons.

episodes.title: Lisa Gets an 'A'
episodes.description: Stuck at home with a cold, Lisa gets addicted to a video game and neglects her homework. Her A+++ on a test wins the school a grant, but when she confesses that she cheated, Skinner covers it up. Meanwhile, Homer raises a lobster.

guests.character: Gavin's Mom / Miss Hoover / Helen Lovejoy (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions like quicksand.
episodes.notes: This episode marks the first time a main Simpson family member is shown explicitly cheating academically, representing a notable moral complexity rarely explored with Lisa's character.

episodes.title: Homer Simpson in: 'Kidney Trouble'
episodes.description: When Grandpa needs a new kidney, Homer agrees to donate one, but then chickens out.
episodes.notes: This episode marks one of the few times Homer's cowardice has genuine moral consequences, as his failure to donate a kidney to his own father represents a darker turn for the character than typical sitcom selfishness.

episodes.title: Mayored to the Mob
episodes.description: After a riot at a sci-fi convention, Mayor Quimby hires Homer as his bodyguard, and Homer witnesses the rampant corruption in town politics. When Homer takes a stand, he runs afoul of the Springfield mafia.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch which sinks into the floor, then rises back up as a whack-a-mole game where each family member gets bonked on the head.
episodes.parody_of: The Bodyguard and various mafia/crime films
episodes.notes: Features the first major appearance of Fat Tony's Springfield mafia operation as central antagonists, establishing the crime family dynamic that would recur throughout the series.

episodes.title: Viva Ned Flanders
episodes.description: When clean-living Ned Flanders wants to experience a new side of life, Homer takes him to Las Vegas. After a drunken binge, they marry a pair of cocktail waitresses.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets crushed by a giant foot from Monty Python's Flying Circus.
episodes.notes: Ned Flanders reveals he's actually 60 years old, having lived an extremely sheltered life that kept him youthful. This episode marks one of the few times the show explicitly states a main character's age, creating continuity issues that fans still debate today.

episodes.title: Wild Barts Can't Be Broken
episodes.description: Scapegoated for the vandalism of drunken Isotopes fans, the children of Springfield wage war on the adults by broadcasting their secrets on the radio.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets bounced high into the air, then fall back down in their underwear.
episodes.notes: This episode features one of the series' most memorable musical numbers with "We Are the Children," a parody of "We Are the World" that showcases the show's ability to blend social commentary with catchy songwriting.

episodes.title: Sunday, Cruddy Sunday
episodes.description: Homer and his friends charter a bus to the Super Bowl, only to find that their tickets are fake. Misadventures ensue.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which is actually a street bench, and they get soaked by a street cleaner truck.
episodes.notes: This Super Bowl episode featured an unprecedented number of celebrity cameos including Dolly Parton, Dan Marino, and Rosey Grier, making it one of the most guest-heavy episodes in the series' history.

episodes.title: Homer to the Max
episodes.description: A new TV series features a suave and debonair character named Homer Simpson. Homer capitalizes on his new namesake, until the character is rewritten to be a bumbling fool. Trying to recapture the positive attention he enjoyed, Homer changes his name to "Max Power."
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch normally, but then the couch and family are sucked into the floor like a drain.
episodes.parody_of: Police drama TV shows and celebrity culture
episodes.notes: The episode introduced Homer's alter ego "Max Power," a name that became a recurring gag and catchphrase throughout the series. This marked one of the show's most direct parodies of celebrity culture and the fickleness of fame in Hollywood.

episodes.title: I'm with Cupid
episodes.description: Apu is making the husbands of Springfield look bad with his extravagent Valentine's Day efforts for Manjula. When the men try to sabotage Apu's grand gesture, they accidentally wind up benefiting from it.

episodes.title: Marge Simpson in: 'Screaming Yellow Honkers'
episodes.description: A new Canyonero SUV turns timid Marge into an aggressive driver, and her road rage lands her in traffic school. But when a stampede occurs at the zoo, the town looks to Marge to save the day.
episodes.notes: The episode introduced the Canyonero, a parody of oversized SUVs that became one of the show's most enduring vehicle gags, complete with a memorable country jingle sung by Hank Williams Jr.

episodes.title: Make Room for Lisa
episodes.description: Indebted from a mishap at the Smithsonian, Homer lets a cell phone company put a transmitter in the house. Forced to share a room with Bart, Lisa resents Homer until a New Age experience gives her empathy.
episodes.notes: This episode marked one of the earliest mainstream television portrayals of concerns about cell phone radiation and health effects, airing during the initial wave of public anxiety about the technology's safety.

episodes.title: Maximum Homerdrive
episodes.description: After beating Homer in a steak-eating competition, a trucker dies; Homer and Bart take over his rig to complete his shipment.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which is actually a street bench, and they get soaked by a bus driving through a puddle.
episodes.parody_of: Trucker/road movies and trucking culture
episodes.notes: This episode features the first appearance of the truck stop waitress who calls Homer "Big Daddy," and includes one of the series' most elaborate trucker culture parodies with authentic CB radio slang throughout.

episodes.title: Simpsons Bible Stories
episodes.description: When Reverend Lovejoy subjects the congregation to an extended Bible reading, the Simpsons fall asleep and have dreams based on Bible stories.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets folded into a greeting card that says 'Greetings from the Simpsons.'
episodes.parody_of: Biblical stories/Bible
episodes.notes: This anthology episode marked one of the few times The Simpsons directly parodied religious content, drawing criticism from some Christian groups while being praised by others for its respectful treatment of Biblical narratives.

episodes.title: Mom and Pop Art
episodes.description: Homer's failed do-it-yourself project gets mistaken for "outsider art." Suddenly in the limelight, Homer must come up with new "works" to impress the art world.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch normally, but then the couch and family are revealed to be a painting in an art gallery.
episodes.parody_of: The art world and outsider art movement
episodes.notes: Features Jasper Johns as himself, marking one of the few times a major contemporary artist appeared on The Simpsons to comment on the art world being satirized.

episodes.title: The Old Man and the 'C' Student
episodes.description: When Bart ruins Springfield's chance to host the Olympics, the kids are sentenced to community service work at the retirement home. To help the residents recapture their youth, Bart takes them on an ill-fated boat ride.
episodes.notes: This episode features one of the series' most devastating endings, with multiple elderly characters drowning in a boat accident that Bart directly caused, marking an unusually dark turn even for later-season Simpsons.

episodes.title: Monty Can't Buy Me Love
episodes.description: A new mogul wins the hearts of the townspeople with a shower of cash. Jealous, Mr. Burns asks Homer to help make him a beloved billionaire too, and they hatch a plan to bring the Loch Ness Monster to Springfield.
episodes.notes: This episode marks the only appearance of Arthur Fortune, the billionaire rival who showers Springfield with money, establishing him as one of the show's most memorable one-off antagonists to Mr. Burns.

episodes.title: They Saved Lisa's Brain
episodes.description: Mayor Quimby flees Springfield, leaving the town chapter of Mensa in charge. As the group's new power goes to their heads, they take their utopian plans too far and the townspeople revolt.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into cushions, then shot out through the back as cubes.
episodes.parody_of: Utopian literature and intellectual dystopias like Brave New World
episodes.notes: Features Stephen Hawking's first and only appearance on The Simpsons, where he delivers the famous line "Your theory of a donut-shaped universe is intriguing, Homer. I may have to steal it.

episodes.title: Thirty Minutes Over Tokyo
episodes.description: After their bank account is drained at a cyber-cafe, the Simpsons go on a disastrous low-budget trip to Japan, where they wind up as contestants on a humiliating game show.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the TV, channel surfing through various shows.
episodes.parody_of: Japanese game shows and travel culture
episodes.notes: This episode marked The Simpsons' first extended international setting outside of brief vacation scenes, requiring extensive research into Japanese culture and customs. The show's parody of bizarre Japanese game shows helped introduce American audiences to the concept years before such programs gained mainstream Western popularity.

episodes.title: Beyond Blunderdome
episodes.description: Homer befriends Mel Gibson just as Mel is completing a remake of Mr. Smith Goes To Washington. Homer convinces Mel to make the film more violent, with disastrous results.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets crushed by a large foot in a Monty Python-style animation.
episodes.parody_of: Mr. Smith Goes to Washington
episodes.notes: Mel Gibson voiced himself in what would become one of his final major mainstream media appearances before his career controversies, making this a time capsule of late 90s Hollywood celebrity culture.

episodes.title: Brother's Little Helper
episodes.description: When Bart commits an extremely destructive prank, he is given Focusyn, a pill to help him concentrate in school. At first the results are impressive, and then Bart starts believing in a conspiracy involving spy satellites and major league baseball.

guests.character: Milhouse Van Houten / Rod Flanders / Jimbo Jones (voice)

episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets folded into the couch which transforms into a foosball table.
episodes.parody_of: A Beautiful Mind and conspiracy thriller films
episodes.notes: This episode marked one of the earliest mainstream TV critiques of ADHD medication in children, airing during the height of Ritalin controversies in the late 1990s.

episodes.title: Guess Who's Coming to Criticize Dinner?
episodes.description: With Lisa's help, Homer uses his vast knowledge of food to become the town's leading restaurant critic. But when he gets petty and vindictive in his criticisms, all the other chefs in town try to kill him.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions, then shot out through the back.
episodes.notes: Features Homer's memorable food critic catchphrase "So good!" which became a running gag throughout his brief culinary journalism career.

episodes.title: Treehouse of Horror X
episodes.description: In "I Know What You Diddily-Iddly-Did" the Simpsons fear they have killed Ned Flanders. "Desperately Xeeking Xena" satirizes superheroes of yore with "Stretch Dude" Bart and "Clobber Girl" Lisa. And "Life's A Glitch, Then You Die" depicts the upcoming horror of Y2K.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch which is actually the tongue of a giant monster that swallows them.
episodes.parody_of: I Know What You Did Last Summer, X-Men/superheroes, Y2K panic
episodes.notes: This marked the first Treehouse of Horror to air in the new millennium and was the show's direct commentary on Y2K hysteria, featuring one of the series' most topical segments that captured the genuine cultural panic of late 1999.

episodes.title: E-I-E-I-(Annoyed Grunt)
episodes.description: Fleeing from a duel, Homer takes his family to live on a farm. There he discovers that by mixing tomatoes, tobacco and radioactivity from the nuclear plant, he can create a dangerously delicious new substance.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which is actually a slot machine that spins them around before they land back in place.
episodes.parody_of: Green Acres
episodes.notes: This episode introduces "tomacco," a fictional hybrid crop that became one of The Simpsons' most memorable running gags and was later actually created by real-life botanist Rob Baur in 2003.

episodes.title: Hello Gutter, Hello Fadder
episodes.description: Homer bowls a perfect 300 game, then concludes that the greatest accomplishment of his life is behind him. He reconnects with the world by bonding with baby Maggie.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch normally, but then the couch and family slide backward through the wall and end up in the neighbor's living room where Ned Flanders is watching TV.
episodes.notes: This episode marks one of the rare times Homer achieves genuine athletic excellence, as his perfect 300 bowling game represents the highest possible score in the sport. The storyline explores Homer's midlife crisis through the lens of peak achievement, making it one of the more introspective character studies in the series.

episodes.title: Eight Misbehavin'
episodes.description: Due to overuse of fertility medication, Apu and wife Manjula give birth to octuplets. Desperate for money, Apu loans the babies to an unscrupulous carny promoter, then needs Homer's help to get them back.
episodes.notes: This episode introduced Apu and Manjula's octuplets, who would become recurring characters throughout the series' run. The babies were named after classic American brands: Lincoln, Freedom, Govt, Cory, Q*bert, Morgan, IKEA, and Nabendu.

episodes.title: Take My Wife, Sleaze
episodes.description: After winning a motorcycle at a dance contest, Homer becomes part of a motorcycle gang. But when he puts Marge's photo in a biker magazine, a much tougher gang takes her, and he must win her back.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets folded into the couch which turns into a pull-out bed.
episodes.parody_of: Biker gang movies and motorcycle culture
episodes.notes: This episode features Homer's motorcycle gang "Hell's Satans," whose name became a recurring joke in later seasons whenever the show referenced amateur or wannabe biker culture.

episodes.title: Grift of the Magi
episodes.description: In this Christmas-themed episode, the students of Springfield Elementary are unwittingly used as a focus group to test new toys. The product of their labors, a cuddly doll named Funzo, is a lot more sinister than he appears.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch as normal, but then the couch and family are revealed to be a dollhouse being played with by a giant Maggie.
episodes.parody_of: How the Grinch Stole Christmas
episodes.notes: This Christmas episode features Tim Robbins as the villainous toy company executive Jim Hope, delivering one of the most memorable guest performances of Season 11. The episode's climactic scene parodies How the Grinch Stole Christmas with Bart destroying the Funzo dolls in a sequence that mirrors the Grinch's change of heart.

episodes.title: Little Big Mom
episodes.description: When Marge leaves the house to recuperate from a skating injury, everything goes to hell - until Lisa convinces Homer and Bart they have leprosy.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets vacuumed up by a giant vacuum cleaner.
episodes.parody_of: The Parent Trap
episodes.notes: Lisa's elaborate deception using fake medical books and staged symptoms marks one of the few times a Simpson child successfully manipulates the entire family for their own good.

episodes.title: Faith Off
episodes.description: Homer gets a bucket stuck on his head, and when it is removed the town is convinced that Bart has the powers of a faith healer. Then mobster Fat Tony tries to turn the boy's spiritual abilities into more sinister directions.
episodes.parody_of: faith healing televangelists and religious revival meetings

episodes.title: The Mansion Family
episodes.description: Worried about his health, Mr. Burns visits the Mayo Clinic. He lets Homer housesit his mansion, which leads of course to an offshore knife fight and an attack by Chinese pirates.
episodes.notes: Mr. Burns' medical examination reveals he has every disease known to man, but they're all perfectly balanced in a state he calls "Three Stooges Syndrome" — creating one of the show's most memorable medical gags.

episodes.title: Saddlesore Galactica
episodes.description: Homer and Bart befriend a diving horse at a county fair. They turn it into a racehorse with an attitude, whose success runs them afoul of mysterious, tree-dwelling jockeys.
episodes.parody_of: Horse racing films and fantasy adventure stories
episodes.notes: This episode marked one of the most polarizing moments in Simpsons history when it abruptly shifted from horse racing satire to fantasy adventure with magical jockey creatures, widely cited by fans as an early example of the show's creative decline.

episodes.title: Alone Again, Natura-Diddily
episodes.description: Ned's wife Maude is inadvertently killed by a T-shirt accident at an auto race. Homer and his family try to help Ned cope.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the TV, where they appear on various channels.
episodes.notes: The shocking death of Maude Flanders was written into the show because voice actress Maggie Roswell was in a pay dispute and couldn't continue recording from her home in Denver.

episodes.title: Missionary: Impossible
episodes.description: When Homer fails to give money he promised over the phone, he is forced by PBS stars to flee the country. He becomes a missionary on a small tropical island, where he introduces the natives to casino gambling.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch which is actually a wooden bench, and they're revealed to be in a church confessional booth.
episodes.parody_of: Missionary films and South Pacific tropical island stories
episodes.notes: This episode served as the season finale and fundraising special for FOX, breaking the fourth wall when actual PBS stars like Betty White and Big Bird chase Homer, blending real public television with the show's fictional world.

episodes.title: Pygmoelian
episodes.description: Plastic surgery gives Moe the bartender a handsome new face and a whole new life as a soap opera star.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets absorbed into the cushions, then ejected back out.
episodes.parody_of: soap operas and celebrity culture
episodes.notes: The episode's title "Pygmoelian" is a clever portmanteau of "Pygmalion" (the Greek myth about transforming someone's appearance) and Moe's name, representing one of the series' most linguistically creative episode titles.

episodes.title: Bart to the Future
episodes.description: We see a possible future in which Bart is a grown-up slacker, living with Ralph Wiggum, while Lisa is no less than President of the United States.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which turns into a slot machine that spins and shows different combinations of the family members.
episodes.notes: This episode famously predicted that Donald Trump would become President of the United States, with Lisa referencing inheriting "quite a budget crunch from President Trump" — a joke that became reality 16 years later.

episodes.title: Days of Wine and D'oh'ses
episodes.description: After being embarrassed by a drunken video of himself, Barney vows to sober up. Newly clean, he gets work as a helicopter pilot.
episodes.parody_of: Days of Wine and Roses
episodes.notes: This episode marked Barney's most significant character development, transforming him from town drunk to responsible helicopter pilot in a rare moment of genuine growth for a secondary character.

episodes.title: Kill the Alligator and Run
episodes.description: After a terrible bout with insomnia, Homer takes the family to Florida during spring break. There, they kill a beloved alligator and are sentenced to a chain gang.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which is actually a cardboard cutout that falls over backwards.
episodes.parody_of: Cool Hand Luke and chain gang prison films
episodes.notes: This episode marks the first time the entire Simpson family was arrested together and sentenced to hard labor, featuring an unusually dark turn even by later-season standards.

episodes.title: Last Tap Dance in Springfield
episodes.description: Lisa enters a tap dancing academy, where the teacher is a child star who never quite grew up. Meanwhile, Homer gets laser eye surgery.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch as normal, but then the couch and family slide backward through the wall and outside the house.
episodes.parody_of: Fame and child star culture

episodes.title: It's a Mad, Mad, Mad, Mad Marge
episodes.description: Otto's girlfriend seems to be usurping Marge's role when she moves in with the family after a dispute over heavy metal.
episodes.parody_of: Single White Female
episodes.notes: This episode marks the only appearance of Becky, Otto's psychotic girlfriend who infiltrates the Simpson household in a parody of the 1992 thriller "Single White Female.

episodes.title: Behind the Laughter
episodes.description: A "Behind the Music"-style look at the Simpsons, narrated by VH1's Jim Forbes, shows their rise to stardom--and the "private hell" that followed.
episodes.parody_of: VH1's Behind the Music
episodes.notes: This mockumentary format broke the fourth wall more completely than any previous Simpsons episode, with the actual VH1 narrator Jim Forbes providing commentary on the fictional Simpson family as if they were real celebrities.

episodes.title: Treehouse of Horror XI
episodes.description: This year's Halloween story sees The Simpsons as "The Munsters" (with everyone except Lisa getting killed), Homer as a wandering spirit on the hunt for a good deed in "G-G-Ghost D-D-Dad," Bart and Lisa as peasants in a fairy tale forest in "Scary Tales Can Come True," and Lisa inadvertently dooming mankind by rescuing a dolphin in "Night of the Dolphin."
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch as skeletons, then their flesh grows back onto their bones.
episodes.parody_of: The Munsters, fairy tales, and nature revenge films
episodes.notes: The dolphin segment's climactic scene where dolphins emerge from the ocean en masse was one of the most expensive animation sequences in Treehouse of Horror history, requiring extensive crowd animation techniques typically reserved for feature films.

episodes.title: A Tale of Two Springfields
episodes.description: While calling Animal Control over a badger taking residence in Santa's Little Helper's doghouse, Homer discovers that Springfield has two different area codes–and ends up leading a revolt that splits the town in two.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets crushed by a giant foot in a Monty Python-style animation.
episodes.parody_of: West Side Story
episodes.notes: This episode features one of the series' most elaborate musical sequences, with a full West Side Story parody complete with choreographed dance numbers that required significantly more animation resources than typical episodes.

episodes.title: Insane Clown Poppy
episodes.description: Krusty takes parenting lessons from Homer when, during an outdoor book fair, a girl tells Krusty that she is his long-lost daughter from a one-night stand with a female soldier who fought during the first Gulf War.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch as normal, but then the couch and family slide backward through the wall and end up outside behind the house.
episodes.notes: First appearance of Sophie, Krusty's daughter, who would become a recurring character representing his attempts at genuine fatherhood throughout the series.

episodes.title: Lisa the Tree Hugger
episodes.description: Lisa falls for the teenaged leader of a militant environmentalist group and tries to impress him by living in Springfield's oldest tree in order to keep it from being cut down.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and sinks into it as if it were quicksand.

episodes.title: Homer vs. Dignity
episodes.description: When the Simpsons once again have financial problems, Mr. Burns pays Homer to play pranks on others and humiliate himself in public. Meanwhile, Smithers creates a Malibu Stacy-themed musical.

episodes.title: The Computer Wore Menace Shoes
episodes.description: Homer decides to buy a computer after turning up to the Plant to find it closed by e-mail. He then creates his own website and it eventually gains attention when he posts gossip. Homer dubs himself as "Mr. X" to conceal his identity. When he reveals that he is Mr. X, he becomes drugged into a mysterious island where people who know too much are imprisoned after Homer starts writing conspiracy theories such as flu shots.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which is actually a giant hand that closes around them.
episodes.parody_of: The Prisoner
episodes.notes: This episode marked one of the earliest mainstream TV depictions of internet blogging and conspiracy theory websites, predating the social media age by several years.

episodes.title: The Great Money Caper
episodes.description: Homer and Bart become father–son con artists (after Bart is given money out of pity when Homer decided to not take him home), but soon find that they are the ones being had.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which is actually a giant hand that grabs them.
episodes.parody_of: The Sting and con artist films
episodes.notes: The episode features an elaborate con artist plot structure that directly homages "The Sting," complete with multiple fake-outs and reveals that the marks were actually running a larger scheme all along.

episodes.title: Skinner's Sense of Snow
episodes.description: A snowstorm traps the students of Springfield Elementary inside, with them overthrowing Principal Skinner when he uses his Army skills to control them. Meanwhile, Homer tries to rescue the children–using Flanders' car.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions one by one.
episodes.parody_of: Lord of the Flies
episodes.notes: This episode features one of the series' most elaborate Lord of the Flies parodies, with Bart leading a student rebellion that transforms the school into a primitive society complete with tribal face paint and spear-wielding guards.

episodes.title: HOMЯ
episodes.description: While working as a human guinea pig (to pay off the family's lost savings after making a bad investment), Homer discovers the root cause of his subnormal intelligence: a crayon that was lodged in his brain ever since he was a boy. He decides to have it removed to increase his IQ, but discovers that being smart does not necessarily equal being happy.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the cushions, then shot out through the back.
episodes.parody_of: Flowers for Algernon
episodes.notes: This episode's title uses the Cyrillic letter "Я" (ya) instead of "R" to spell "HOMЯ," making it one of the few Simpsons episodes to use non-Latin characters in its title.

episodes.title: Pokey Mom
episodes.description: Marge tries to rehabilitate a convict with remarkable artistic talent. Meanwhile, Homer uses a trash can to fix people's spines.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into the TV, where they appear in various shows including a nature documentary.
episodes.notes: The episode introduces Jack Crowley, a one-time convict character whose artistic talents and tragic backstory made him one of the most memorable single-episode guest characters in the series' middle years.

episodes.title: Worst Episode Ever
episodes.description: Bart and Milhouse are banned from The Android Dungeon after stopping Comic Book Guy from buying a box of priceless Star Wars memorabilia for $5, but are hired as his replacements when Comic Book Guy has a heart attack and is advised to leave his job in order to make friends.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which is actually a player piano that plays a tune while the family bounces up and down.
episodes.notes: The episode's title became an ironic rallying cry among fans who felt the show's quality was declining in later seasons, making it one of the most meta-textually referenced episode titles in Simpsons history.

episodes.title: Tennis the Menace
episodes.description: While making funeral arrangements for Grampa's future, the funeral planner pitches the idea of a mausoleum that uses the same amount of cement as a full-sized tennis court. This prompts Homer to instead build a tennis court in his backyard, initially mistaking the sport for "Foxy Boxing". The tennis court makes Marge and Homer popular in town, but it turns out that this is only because Homer's inferior tennis ability makes them an easy target. Bart on the other hand, shines as a natural.

episodes.title: Day of the Jackanapes
episodes.description: Krusty announces his retirement due to interference from network executives and the growing popularity of the big-money game show "Me Wantee", but when Krusty reveals to Sideshow Bob that all of the episodes featuring him have been erased, Sideshow Bob uses Krusty's biggest fan (Bart) to murder him during Krusty's farewell show.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into a black hole that appears in the center cushion.
episodes.parody_of: The Manchurian Candidate
episodes.notes: This episode marked Sideshow Bob's return after a four-season absence, with Kelsey Grammer reprising his role for what many consider the character's final truly menacing appearance before becoming more of a comic figure.

episodes.title: New Kids on the Blecch
episodes.description: A music producer selects Bart, Nelson, Milhouse and Ralph to be members of the next hit boy band, who record subliminal songs about joining the Navy.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch as normal, but then the couch and family are revealed to be a dollhouse being played with by a giant Maggie.
episodes.parody_of: Boy bands like *NSYNC and Backstreet Boys
episodes.notes: The boy band "Party Posse" featured guest voices from *NSYNC members Justin Timberlake, JC Chasez, Chris Kirkpatrick, and Joey Fatone as themselves. This marked one of the first times a major pop group appeared on The Simpsons during the height of their fame, just as the boy band craze was peaking.

episodes.title: Hungry, Hungry Homer
episodes.description: Homer becomes a Good Samaritan after discovering the simple joys of helping people in need–which is put to the test when he goes on a hunger strike after the owner of the Springfield Isotopes attempts to discredit him when Homer stumbles on his plot to discreetly move the team to Albuquerque, New Mexico.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch, which is actually a giant sandwich that gets eaten by a larger Homer.
episodes.parody_of: Gandhi and hunger strike protest movements
episodes.notes: This episode features Homer's most sustained act of civil disobedience, with his hunger strike lasting longer than any previous protest in the series. The Albuquerque Isotopes later became the name of a real minor league baseball team, making this one of the few times The Simpsons directly inspired a professional sports franchise.

episodes.title: Bye Bye Nerdie
episodes.description: When Lisa becomes the target of a female bully, Lisa discovers a scientific reason as to why bullies pick on nerds. Meanwhile, Homer goes into the baby-proofing business, which is threatening the businesses of pediatricians and greeting card salesmen.

guests.character: Milhouse Van Houten / Jimbo Jones / Janey Powell (voice)

episodes.title: Simpson Safari
episodes.description: When the Simpsons run out of food thanks to a bag boy strike (caused by Homer), the family finds an old box of animal crackers with a sweepstakes that sends The Simpsons to Africa.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch which is actually a giant slice of bread, and they become part of a sandwich as another slice comes down from above.
episodes.parody_of: African safari adventure films and nature documentaries

episodes.title: Trilogy of Error
episodes.description: Homer's rush to the hospital to reattach his thumb, Lisa's rush to school to win the science fair, and Bart's run-in with an illegal fireworks scheme are interconnected in a parody of Go and Run Lola Run.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets crushed flat by a giant foot from Monty Python's Flying Circus.
episodes.parody_of: Go and Run Lola Run
episodes.notes: This groundbreaking episode was the first Simpsons story told from multiple interconnected perspectives, directly parodying the innovative narrative structure of German film "Run Lola Run" and influencing later animated shows to experiment with non-linear storytelling.

episodes.title: I'm Goin' to Praiseland
episodes.description: After finding a sketchbook belonging to his late wife Maude, Ned Flanders opens a Bible-themed amusement park to honor her memory.
episodes.parody_of: Christian theme parks and religious entertainment venues
episodes.notes: This episode marks the final major storyline centered around Maude Flanders' death, providing closure to the character arc that began when Maggie Roswell left the show due to a salary dispute.

episodes.title: Children of a Lesser Clod
episodes.description: After spraining his knee during a basketball game, Homer begins taking care of the neighborhood kids to cure his boredom, prompting jealousy from Bart and Lisa, who feel that Homer is giving the kids the attention they never had.
episodes.couch_gag: The family sits on the couch and gets sucked into a black hole that appears in the center cushion.

episodes.title: Simpsons Tall Tales
episodes.description: When Homer refuses to pay a five dollar airport tax to fly to Delaware, the family is forced to ride in a livestock car of a train instead. There they meet a singing hobo who tells three tall tales:  Homer as Paul Bunyan.  Lisa as Connie Appleseed, a parody of Johnny Appleseed.  Bart as Tom Sawyer with Nelson as Huckleberry Finn.
episodes.parody_of: American tall tales and folk legends
episodes.notes: Features the first and only appearance of the singing hobo character, voiced by Hank Azaria, who serves as the episode's narrator and performs original folk songs between each tall tale segment.
